title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
description: Implement a trie (prefix tree) that supports three operations: `insert(word)` adds `word` to the trie, `search(word)` returns `true` if and only if `word` was previously inserted as a complete word, and `startsWith(prefix)` returns `true` if and only if some previously inserted word has `prefix` as a prefix. You are given an array `ops` where each entry is `[opName, arg]`. Return an array of results in order — `null` for each `insert`, and a boolean for each `search` and `startsWith`.

examples.explanation: After inserting 'apple', 'app' is a prefix but not a complete word until inserted.

hints: Har node me children map (object) aur ek isEnd flag rakh.
search aur startsWith dono walk karte hain — fark sirf last node ke isEnd check pe hai.
Insert me last node ka isEnd true mark kar.

title: Longest Common Prefix
description: Given an array of strings `words`, return the longest string that is a common prefix of all of them. If there is no common prefix, or the array is empty, return the empty string `""`.
hints: Trie banake root se chal — jab tak ek hi child hai aur current node end nahi hai.
Simpler: vertical scan — har column pe check kar saare words me same char hai ya nahi.
Empty array ya empty string mile toh '' return kar.

title: Implement Trie II (Prefix Tree)
description: Implement a trie that supports three operations: `insert(word)` adds `word` to the trie, `countWordsEqualTo(word)` returns the number of times the exact `word` was inserted, and `countWordsStartingWith(prefix)` returns the number of inserted words that have `prefix` as a prefix. You are given an array `ops` where each entry is `[opName, arg]`. Return an array of results in order — `null` for each `insert`, and an integer for each count operation.
hints: Har trie node pe do counters rakh: `endCount` (kitne words isi node pe end hue) aur `prefixCount` (kitne words is path se guzre).
Insert me path pe har node ka prefixCount++ aur final node ka endCount++.
Walk fail karo prefix me missing char par → return 0.

title: Replace Words
description: You are given a list of strings `roots` and a string `sentence` whose words are separated by single spaces. For every word in `sentence`, if one or more strings in `roots` is a prefix of that word, replace the word with the shortest such root; otherwise leave the word unchanged. Return the resulting sentence.

examples.input: roots = ["cat","bat","rat"], sentence = "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
examples.output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

constraints: Lowercase English letters and single spaces
hints: Saare roots ko ek trie me daal de.
Har word pe trie walk kar — pehla isEnd node hi shortest root hai.
Walk break ho jaye toh original word rakh.

args: the cattle was rattled by the battery
tests.expected: the cat was rat by the bat
tests.label: classic

title: Counting Words With a Given Prefix
description: You are given an array of strings `names` and an array of query strings `prefixes`. For each prefix in `prefixes`, count how many strings in `names` start with that prefix, and return the counts in the same order as `prefixes`. Duplicate strings in `names` are counted independently (each occurrence counts).
hints: Trie banate waqt har visited node pe ek `count` increment kar.
Query: prefix walk kar; node mile toh uska count return kar, varna 0.
Same name multiple times insert hone par count properly badhega.

title: Top K Frequent Prefix Suggestions
description: You are given an array of strings `hashtags`, where repeated occurrences of a string denote its frequency, and a string `prefix`. Return up to 3 distinct strings from `hashtags` that have `prefix` as a prefix, ordered by frequency in descending order, breaking ties lexicographically (smallest string first). If no string matches, return an empty array.
hints: Frequency map of distinct hashtags + trie of distinct hashtags. Query: walk to prefix node, DFS collect all words below, sort by (-count, word).
Top 3 ke liye full sort thik hai chote inputs me; bada ho toh min-heap of size 3 use kar.
Empty prefix valid hai — root se DFS poore trie pe.

title: Word Break
description: Given a string `s` and a list of strings `dictionary`, return `true` if `s` can be segmented into a sequence of one or more strings from `dictionary`. The same dictionary word may be reused any number of times in the segmentation.

examples.explanation: 'a'+'b'+'cd' or 'abc'+'d'? d not in dict — try 'a'+'b'+'cd'.

hints: DP: `dp[i]` = can suffix starting at index i be segmented? `dp[s.length]=true`.
From index i, walk trie char-by-char; jab kabhi `isEnd` mile aur `dp[j+1]` true ho, toh `dp[i] = true`.
Memoize ya iterate i from right to left.

title: Word Search II
description: Given an `m x n` grid of lowercase letters `board` and a list of strings `words`, return all strings in `words` that can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where adjacent cells are horizontally or vertically neighboring. The same cell may not be used more than once within a single word. Return the matched words in lexicographic order, with no duplicates.
constraints: board[i][j] and words[i] are lowercase English
hints: Saare words ka trie banake board pe DFS chala — trie node se chal, har step pe board cell aur trie child match honi chahiye.
Word mil jaye toh result me daal aur trie node ka isEnd false set kar (avoid duplicate).
Visited cells track kar; backtrack pe restore.

title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array
description: Given an array of non-negative integers `nums` of length at least 2, return the maximum value of `nums[i] ^ nums[j]`, where `^` is the bitwise XOR operation and `i != j`.
hints: Bit trie: 31 down to 0, har number ka bit insert kar.
Har number ke liye trie traverse kar opposite bit prefer karte hue — yahi maximum XOR banata hai.
Insert pehle, then query har element ke liye, max track.

title: Concatenated Words
description: Given a list of distinct lowercase strings `words`, return all strings that are concatenated words. A concatenated word is a string that can be formed by joining at least two shorter strings from `words` (each chosen string may be used multiple times). Return the result sorted lexicographically.
constraints: All words are distinct lowercase English strings
hints: Trie of all words. Har word ke liye check kar: kya use 2+ trie words me todha ja sakta hai?
DP per word: from index i, walk trie; jab isEnd mile aur (j == word.length OR suffix(j) similarly decomposable), then yes.
Same word ko apne andar use mat karna — depth >= 1 word use ho chuka ho ya word khud trie me ho but recursion suffix pe continue ho rahi ho.

title: Search Suggestions System
description: Given a list of strings `products` and a string `searchWord`, return a list of lists where the i-th list contains up to 3 lexicographically smallest strings from `products` that share `searchWord[0..i]` as a prefix (i.e. the first `i + 1` characters of `searchWord`). Once no product matches the current prefix, the remaining entries are empty lists. The result has length equal to `searchWord.length`.

examples.explanation: Last char 'x' has no match, so empty after that — but search runs full length.

hints: Pehle products ko sort kar de — phir trie me daal; har visited node pe pehle 3 products store kar (lex smallest naturally).
Query me prefix walk kar; dead hone par baaki sab empty arrays.
Trie ka beauty: suggestions list pre-baked hai, query bas walk hai.

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
description: Implement a word dictionary supporting two operations: `addWord(word)` inserts `word`, and `search(word)` returns `true` if and only if some previously added word matches `word`. The search string may contain the character `.`, which matches any single lowercase letter. You are given an array `ops` where each entry is `[opName, arg]`. Return an array of results in order — `null` for each `addWord`, and a boolean for each `search`.

examples.output: [null, null, null, false, true, true, true]

constraints: word in addWord is lowercase English
word in search may contain `.` wildcard
hints: Trie + DFS recursion. Normal char: drill down ek child me. `.` ka matlab: saare children pe try kar.
Base case: idx == word.length → node.isEnd return kar.
Wildcard worst case slow ho sakta hai, par average pe theek hai.

title: Map Sum Pairs
description: Implement a structure that maps string keys to integer values and supports two operations: `insert(key, val)` sets the value for `key`, overwriting any previous value for the same key, and `sum(prefix)` returns the sum of values over all keys that have `prefix` as a prefix. You are given an array `ops` where each entry describes one operation. Return an array of results in order — `null` for each `insert`, and an integer for each `sum`.

examples.explanation: Re-insert overwrites; sum reflects the new value only.

hints: Har trie node pe ek `sum` field rakh — path pe har node ka sum += value during insert.
Re-insert handle karne ke liye purana value yaad rakh aur delta apply kar (newVal - oldVal).
sum(prefix) = walk to prefix node; uska sum return kar (0 if walk fails).

title: Stream of Characters
description: You are given a dictionary of strings `words` and an input string `letters` whose characters arrive one at a time. Return an array of booleans of the same length as `letters`, where index `i` is `true` if and only if, after consuming `letters[0..i]`, some suffix of the stream ending exactly at position `i` equals one of the strings in `words`.
hints: Trick: dictionary words ko REVERSE karke trie banao. Stream me jo bhi aaye, latest char se peeche jaake walk karo.
Buffer me saare chars rakh; query pe end se start tak walk — `isEnd` mile to true.
Walk break ho jaye to stop kar do (no longer matching).

title: Palindrome Pairs
description: Given an array of distinct lowercase strings `words`, return all index pairs `[i, j]` with `i != j` such that the concatenation `words[i] + words[j]` is a palindrome. Return the pairs sorted in ascending order by `i`, and then by `j`.
constraints: words[i] are distinct lowercase English strings (empty string allowed)
hints: Reverse-trie banao saare words ka. Har word w ke liye, trie pe walk karo jaise w match karna ho us word ke reverse se.

Do cases handle karo: (A) trie me kuch word khatam ho gaya bich me — to baaki w ka suffix palindrome ho, (B) w pura khatam ho gaya — to is node ke neeche jo bhi words hain unka extra prefix palindromic ho.
Per-node `palindromeBelow` list rakhna helps Case B fast.

title: Short Encoding of Words
description: Given a list of strings `words`, return the minimum length of a reference string that encodes every word, where each word is recoverable as a substring that starts at some index and ends at a `#` terminator. A word may be omitted from the encoding if it is a suffix of another word in the list. The returned length must include one trailing `#` for every encoded word.

examples.explanation: S = "time#bell#" of length 10. "me" is a suffix of "time" so it shares.

hints: Idea: agar koi word X kisi aur word Y ka suffix hai to X ko encoding me alag se rakhne ki zaroorat nahi.
Reverse-trie banao saare DISTINCT words ka. Har word ka end-node check kar — agar uske children hain to wo dusre word ka suffix hai (skip).
Leaf end-nodes contribute (length + 1) (1 for `#`).

title: Longest Word in Dictionary
description: Given a list of lowercase strings `words`, return the longest string `w` in `words` such that every prefix of `w` of length 1, 2, ..., |w| - 1 also appears in `words`. If there are multiple answers, return the lexicographically smallest one. If no such string exists, return the empty string.

examples.explanation: "apple" and "apply" both work; lex smaller is "apple".

constraints: Lowercase English letters; words are distinct
hints: Set me daal saari words. Har word ke liye uske saare prefixes (length 1..len-1) check kar set me hain ya nahi.
Tie-break: pehle length compare, equal ho to lex.
Trie approach bhi chalega — trie me insert kar, phir DFS me sirf un nodes pe jaa jinka isEnd true hai.

title: Number of Distinct Substrings in a String
description: Given a string `s`, return the number of distinct non-empty substrings of `s`.

examples.explanation: a, b, c, ab, bc, abc.

hints: Har suffix ko trie me insert kar — har new node ek new distinct substring represent karta hai.
Counter use kar: jab kabhi naya child banaye, +1.
Time O(n^2) but space optimal for distinct count without suffix automaton complexity.

title: Prefix and Suffix Search
description: You are given a list of strings `words` and a list of `queries`, where each query is a pair `[prefix, suffix]`. For each query, return the largest index `i` such that `words[i]` starts with `prefix` and ends with `suffix`; if no word satisfies both conditions, return `-1`. Return the answers in the same order as `queries`.

examples.explanation: "oyo" starts with o and ends with o; "ola" ends with a; "zomato" starts with z.

hints: Trick: har word `w` ke liye saare (suffix + "#" + word) keys ko trie me daalo. Trie me node pe max-index store karo.
Query (prefix, suffix) → key = suffix + "#" + prefix. Trie me walk; agar mile to node ka max-index return.
Worst case: O(L^2) words, par L <= 10 to OK.

title: Camelcase Matching
description: You are given a list of strings `queries` and a string `pattern`. For each query, return a boolean indicating whether it matches `pattern`. A query matches `pattern` if lowercase letters can be inserted into `pattern` (at any positions, any number of them) so that it becomes exactly equal to the query. In other words, the uppercase letters of the query must equal `pattern`'s uppercase letters in order, and `pattern` must be a subsequence of the query. Return the results in the same order as `queries`.
constraints: queries[i] and pattern consist of English letters
hints: Two-pointer: query pe iterate kar; agar current char pattern[i] hai to i++. Else agar uppercase hai to fail (uppercase ko pattern me match karna pad raha tha).
Loop ke baad i == pattern.length hona chahiye.
Trie-based variant: pattern ko ek trie me daalo (chains of length 1) — par yahan two-pointer simpler hai.
